posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked:
A friend of mine shared with me Pasquet’s thoughts on modern bordeaux.

I am interested of what fellow berserkers and Bordeaux aficionados think about it.

Pasquet has a strong opinion for sure. His project with Liber Pater is really interesting, excluding the prices…

image1170×1429 520 KB

image1170×2252 1020 KB

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
He’s commenting on an article in Le Monde, which you should read for context.

Being diplomatic, one might say that the quality of the response was merited by the quality of the article.

  • It’s worth adding that when Pasquet refers to “Parker and his apostles” he means (I presume) all wine critics who score wines, not just TWA and its alumni.

posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked:
I read the incipit. Didn’t think an article about parkerization was worth the subscription to Le Monde.

A part of me agree with Pasquet about the world of wine journalism, and critics.

On the other side the world is not just black and white. I’m sure that many critics do their job with passion and trying to be as impartial as possible.

I always paved my road of wine discovery with suggestions of mentors and fellow wine lovers.
I understand that not everybody has the opportunity and luck to do that. Wine journalists could have an important role in filling the gap. Most of them fail miserably though.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I don’t disagree. It’s just rather galling when one tries to write about Bordeaux honestly and coherently, from a clear point of view and while going into the region’s agronomic and winemaking technicalities, informed by a deep appreciation for its past; only to be told that one is a liar by a man who makes ludicrously priced Cabernet Sauvignon-based blends, marketed under another name (Petite Vidure), from sub-par terroirs, and who seems to spend more time on social media than in his vineyards.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
It’s interesting to read CT notes about his wines. Here are @Jeff_Leve 's notes about his 2015 and 2011 Liber Pater:

2011 Clearly the most concentrated wine of the vintage, the wine is inky in color. Liber Pater waited until October 10 begin their harvest. In part, that explains the licorice, tobacco, cocoa, and ripe dark fruits. The wine is full bodied, concentrated and leaves with you fresh, black cherries, dark chocolate and cassis in the finish.

2015 Inky dark in color, this wine is thick, rich, dense and intense. The weight takes up a lot of volume on your palate, though due to its freshness, it is not at all flabby. The finish is packed with lush, dark, chocolate covered blackberries and jam. Produced from a blend of 80% Cabernet Sauvignon, 15% Petit Verdot and 5% assorted rare, grape varieties that were used pre phylloxera, including: Castet, Marselan and Tarney Coulant. Due to these non-Bordeaux varietals, the wine will be sold as a Vin de France. Aged in 100% new, French, oak barrels, this wine was made for the first time with 100% ungrafted vines, meaning that in theory, this is about as close as you are going to get to wines produced in the 19th century, albeit with a lot more selection, new oak and low yields. Still, it’s fascinating to consider what is taking place at Liber Pater. 93 - 95 Pts

Of course, I haven’t tried the wines, but reading Jeff’s and other TNs on CT, it doesn’t sound like the wines are nearly as anti-modern as his rhetoric.

posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked:
I completely understand your point of view William.

I don’t know you personally, and I’m not subscribed to the wine advocate, but every post of yours that i stumbled across on wine berserkers, is always insightful, well written, and show your knowledge about wine making.

You defenitely know more about Bordeaux, Pasquet, and Liber Pater than most of us, so your contribution on the topic is really appreciated.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

It’s worth adding that when Pasquet refers to “Parker and his apostles” he means (I presume) all wine critics who score wines, not just TWA and its alumni.

Certainly I agree, as I’ve had arguments with plenty here, even great friends, on the need to put a score (points) on a bottle. The experience, the notes, the story is what compels me, not the numbers/scores.

posts.name: Rich K0rz€nk0
posts.cooked:
Yeah, I agree with the experience. I stopped pointing things some time back for that same reason and a bit of soul searching.

I get his point, it’s rather strong though not wrong, and maybe he should lay off the booze before posting lol. It is a more complicated ecosystem and the dawn of the interwebz and marketing to dumb things down for the broader consumer base would have made much of the state of things inevitable.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: Highly polarizing rhetoric can be highly profitable these days. Just look at cable and internet news and sports sites.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

It’s interesting to read CT notes about his wines. Here are @Jeff_Leve 's notes about his 2015 and 2011 Liber Pater:

2011 Clearly the most concentrated wine of the vintage, the wine is inky in color. Liber Pater waited until October 10 begin their harvest. In part, that explains the licorice, tobacco, cocoa, and ripe dark fruits. The wine is full bodied, concentrated and leaves with you fresh, black cherries, dark chocolate and cassis in the finish.

2015 Inky dark in color, this wine is thick, rich, dense and intense. The weight takes up a lot of volume on your palate, though due to its freshness, it is not at all flabby. The finish is packed with lush, dark, chocolate covered blackberries and jam. Produced from a blend of 80% Cabernet Sauvignon, 15% Petit Verdot and 5% assorted rare, grape varieties that were used pre phylloxera, including: Castet, Marselan and Tarney Coulant. Due to these non-Bordeaux varietals, the wine will be sold as a Vin de France. Aged in 100% new, French, oak barrels, this wine was made for the first time with 100% ungrafted vines, meaning that in theory, this is about as close as you are going to get to wines produced in the 19th century, albeit with a lot more selection, new oak and low yields. Still, it’s fascinating to consider what is taking place at Liber Pater. 93 - 95 Pts

Of course, I haven’t tried the wines, but reading Jeff’s and other TNs on CT, it doesn’t sound like the wines are nearly as anti-modern as his rhetoric.

FWIW, the wines I reviewed were made in an older style for Liber Pater. They were produced from different grape varieties and blends, and were aged in 100% new oak.

Today, Liber Pater uses no new oak and I think all aging takes place in either 100% amphora or a portion is aged in glass wine globes. The varieties used for the blends are also quite different.

I like Loic Pasquet. He’s an interesting go his own way guy with a lot of smart views. Although we often, if not usually disagree. However, we have a nice time disagreeing and enjoy frank discussions.

I have been to his vineyards snd cellars and I’ve tasted recent vintages, but he sets the rules which are I can post notes, without scores. So we taste sometimes, but I don’t take notes as he doesn’t want his wines scored. It’s just for curiosity.

posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked: Perhaps binge drinking 30k$ bottles of liber pater makes you angry and aggressive!

posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked:
He proved his point with the status quo Bordeaux in the end. Commercially in primis.

He sells the most expensive wine in the area.

“Fifteen years ago, Bordeaux didn’t know me; 10 years ago, they laughed at me; four years ago they wanted to kill me, and now they copy me.”

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
GiacomoB:

and now they copy me.

Who is copying him?

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked: maybe he’s referring to the rampant price inflation that Liber Pate has gone through in the last few years

posts.name: Giacomo Buffetti
posts.cooked:
I cited his sentence from an interview that he gave. Actually i have no clue if anybody’s copying him.

Probably just a hyperbole

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
At Millesima the price for a bottle of Liber Pater varies between 3.000 and 4.000 Euros. If he is successful with this business model he has my respect. This would be the proof that almost anything is possible in this crazy world. But ok – there are handbags for ladies that cost 30.000 dollar. So this wine is “relativ cheap”.

In Berlin you can buy “Berlin air in tins”. That works too

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Decanter – 17 Jan 16

Bordeaux Liber Pater winemaker found guilty of fraud  - Decanter

The producer of Bordeaux's rare Liber Pater wine, Loïc Pasquet, has been found guilty of defrauding authorities of almost 600,000 euros in EU funding - but he plans to appeal the ruling...
